speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, March 18th, 1997. Interview with Esther Gever. You were telling us about a neighbor of yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was-- she had a little boy, an only child. He was my friend. She was very beautiful and very well kept, very sophisticated lady. They came. They arrested her and did not take her to a police station. They took her to the street surrounded by women, men, young women, Austrian, and made her do certain things to degrade her, to humiliate her. First, they started with washing the sidewalk. Then they started spitting at her. That wasn't enough. They told her to dance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They told her to jump. They messed up her hair. They threw mud at her. Then finally, they cut her hair. They tore her clothes. And when they had enough, they sent her home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you watching this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw her come home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I would-- we were told what they did to her. She told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was her little boy while this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her little boy was with us. We took him in. And they came to-- when they took her, my mother took him. He was crying. She comforted him. She told him that they probably just want to ask her questions. She will be right back. When they-- when she came back, she went and my mother went in with her, tried to calm her down. I don't know what happened to them. There were so many incidents, so many people disappeared. And schools were separated. All of a sudden the Jewish children were not to contaminate the class, and they were separated. The teachers were very cruel. They purposely wanted to make us feel uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My teacher cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She said she's sorry to see me go. [CRYING] And I-- you'll see, she says. Everything will be OK. The teachers were really-- they were demeaning. They were calling us names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get the new teachers? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have absolutely no idea. I don't know. But they were-- I had the mean-- she was a absolutely terror. She was a terror. She terrorized us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]. All kinds of names to [GERMAN]. Even if something was correct, she would try to confuse. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was no pleasure going to school anymore, but we had to. We were afraid to rock the boat. We just took everything in stride and didn't know what to do. Everyone was planning something. Whoever had relatives abroad, visas, all kinds-- people had that and they were lucky to get out. Our-- my father's family was in Poland, so there was no where-- they wanted us to come, and we would have probably wound up in Poland anyway. But before that, there was a Kristallnacht. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they came from building to building, from apartment to apartment knocking on the doors and pulling out the men. You couldn't object. If you did, they beat you. They were brutal. They came for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being as I was attached to my father, I held his arm and I stood up against my mother's [LAUGHS] upbringing. I said, no, you cannot take my father. And I said, why would you take my father? Why? And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, you will not take him. I won't let him go. He didn't do anything. At that point, he grabbed me and threw me against the wall and I didn't remember anything. I blacked out. I had a concussion. When I came to, my father was gone. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother and my grandmother were standing near me. She was holding my baby sister in her arms. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Our world collapsed completely. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no choice. We weren't alone, but the pain was just unbearable. Everyone was running around, asking questions one another for advice. Where did they take him? And we didn't know. Three weeks, we didn't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three weeks, we were told he's in Dachau. We received the first postcard. A postcard didn't tell us anything, because everything was censored, so certain sentences were blacked out. Only that he's well, and he misses us and he loves us. A month after my father was gone, again, a knock on the door. Knocks on the door still terrify me sometimes. It wasn't knocking-- banging-- and two SS men--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with a young couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it wasn't knocking?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was banging. Banging on the door. They never knocked. They banged. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A young couple. And the SS men came into the house, and he said with that smile, that cold, vile look on his face-- and he said, this couple just got married, and you will provide them with a dowry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The girl looked around the house. She liked it. They proceeded to carry out everything from our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a couple, probably good party members, Nazis. And they took everything. They took everything. The only thing she said is that she does not want the mattress, because she will not-- she won't sleep in a-- on the mattress from a [GERMAN]. And they took every thing. They even took our kitchen furniture, rugs, paintings, silver, crystals, everything. We were afraid to move, because we were-- we just didn't want to make them angry. We didn't object.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother said, they took my husband. How much does that mean to me? Let them take it. They can't-- the most valuable thing they took already.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were standing there for hours until our house was cleaned out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Neighbors brought us some chairs in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister slept in her carriage, because even the baby crib they took. [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We slept on the mattresses on the floor. First roughing of the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Family brought us linens, because they took the bedroom set with everything in it. All the linens, all the clothes, everything. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And then my mother proceeded to-- with being a-- at the time, they were freeing from Dachau or any concentration camp if you had a ticket overseas, so my mother tried to get a ticket to Shanghai for my father to get him out of Dachau. She was at the Gestapo from midnight till all hours of the day, because the lines. Everyone was there for the same reason. The lines were tremendous. And my grandmother stayed with us. At the same time, there were announcements made that children are being taken to England, Jewish children. That families want to-- take care of Jewish children, and they required a picture of the child. And if they like the child, that that Jewish family will then keep that child through the war. Not adopting it, but making it safer and giving it a home. And my mother sent my picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't want to go. I begged her. I said, I would rather die. I don't want to go. I want to be with you. Whatever happens, happens. I don't want to go. And she gave up. She finally let me have my way. Food became scarce for Jews. There was food, but they weren't selling it to Jews. My little sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they go about not selling it to Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just-- if they sold to a Jew, I guess they would be punished. Jewish stores were looted. Jewish Jews, I think stores were taken away. Everything that the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any warning to the Jews not to buy a certain-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] how did you know not to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were allowed to buy bread. They were allowed to buy maybe grain. I don't know. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But no butter or sugar or meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they allowed to patronize the same stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know that. I don't remember. I don't remember that, because I never did the shopping. And we had so many other problems. I really had to become a caretaker overnight. And I-- I know that Frau Kotsl, our neighbor, was helpful to us. She risked her life to bring us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea how she-- she bought it, and she came to us and she gave us the food. She was sneaking in and out of our house. We know that she did this for many other Jewish families. They arrested her husband, and he never lived to see the day after that. No one ever saw him. What they did to him is obvious. They must have sent him to a concentration camp, or maybe they killed him right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whom your mother was approaching for the tickets to Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't-- no, I don't. I know that there was papers required to free my father, and a lot of monies were paid for that ticket because everyone needed one and wanted one. How she got it, I have no idea. I really don't know. And I never asked, because life progressed in such a manner it wasn't important how she got it. She got it, and it was a fact. And when my father was freed from Dachau, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after months of terrible, terrible stress and suffering, he came to the house. I barely recognized him. He was not my father, only resembled my father. It seems that they beat him to a pulp in Dachau. He had a fractured skull. The reason he survived is that they threw him on a pile of snow, and that snow saved his life. It reduced the swelling of the brain or something. I don't know exactly what it was. But he wasn't the same man ever. [SOBBING] Ever. Something was missing. He wasn't able to function the same way. He-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was terrible to look at him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he knew enough not to leave for Shanghai. He said, I'm not going to leave you. He refused to go. And my mother started planning a different escape. We-- after you were freed from Dachau, there was one month given for-- and then you had-- you were exiled. So we had that one month. During that month, my mother made contact with people that contacted smugglers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, for also a certain amount of money, they smuggled us to Poland. But at first, we had to go to Beuthen Bytom where it was a gathering point. Several families would gather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Bytom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bytom is in Germany on the border of-- between Germany and Poland. Austria--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you didn't go through Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think that Czechoslovakia may not have let us in. I don't know. I-- I really don't know. I think that was the agenda-- a lot of German Jews and Austrian Jews took that route. We came to Bytom, and over there we-- it was a gathering point. They gathered us in the room at night in the dark. We started walking. No, first they took us by truck, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gathered you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw the faces of these men. It was so dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: The smugglers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The smugglers. They were civilians. They did that for money. They did not do it for sympathy to the Jews. I never saw their faces because it was so dark. We could really not see faces, only outline of-- physical outline of the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you were supposed to leave Austria within the month after releasing your father from the camp, wasn't there a legal route that you could have gone? How did they want to run the exile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the legal route would have been only overseas, and that was the only ticket available. Departing actually from Austria was very trying, because of my grandmother. We had to leave her. She wouldn't leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: She believed that older people will not be harassed. That old women, who is going to touch me? What can they want from me? What can-- of what-- what would they gain by harassing me? I-- I'll be here. I'll wait for you when you return.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] Anyway, we came to Beuthen Bytom. And at night they took us first by truck, and then we walked. No one was allowed to talk, sneeze, cough. We just held hands like in a chain, and we followed one another. We crossed the border, and we got intercepted by a Polish border patrol. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took us to the station. Next morning, they sent us back to Germany. They didn't let us in, [SIGHS] so we were in hell again. And-- but my father said that we have a chance next night to go again, because they were-- they were responsible for a successful arrival in Poland. And they would not get paid. But they said that this time, we are not to take anything. They will not be responsible if we take anything with us. So we were only with our clothes, the clothes on our backs. And they took a different road through the water at night. They walked through a river. I have no idea where and what and what the name of it was. We walked through a river very quietly as not to make the water splash. You had to go very, very quietly. I was sitting on the shoulders of one smuggler, terrified with my eyes closed. My sister was carried. The clothes were taken off before we reached that border. We took-- they-- everyone undressed in their shoes, and when we crossed, everyone dried up and got dressed quietly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was not that cold. It was chilly, but it was not that cold. Night in cold water. I remember-- I don't know what time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I think, three-- three months before the war with Poland or so. When would that be? I-- I really don't remember months or dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be-- three months before, that would be in June.

speaker: interviewee
text: In June. So it was chilly, but it was not cold. We made a successful arrival in Poland that night. We succeeded. They took us to a farm. We slept over in a barn. The next morning we cleaned up, and someone took us to a railroad station and we went to Krakow. In Krakow, my father had relatives, cousins. He was such a-- and it must have been a weekend. It must have been on a-- on a-- on a Saturday or Friday night, because I remember pictures of narrow streets with a lot of Hasidic people. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. And to me, I was bewildered by that picture. I have never seen it in-- concentrated in one group so many and so free walking the streets and-- and so relaxed with Shabbes afternoon and-- and happy. And-- and we arrived at those cousins. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the neighborhood have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't-- I did not speak Polish. Don't-- you know, it was very hard for me. I observed everything visually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the cousins you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I don't remember. We slept one night, and we proceeded. I know they were very warm and very loving and very nice, but I can't remember. We took a train to Rozwadow where my father's parents, my grandparents lived, and we arrived very early morning there. There wasn't a living soul out in the street. The greeting of my-- my grandfather and an uncle of mine came to the station. It's the first time I saw my grandfather, and he was so beautiful. He had a white, long beard, and he had-- and an aura of godliness. He was so warm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he hasn't seen my father for a number of years, so son and father [SOBBING] met. It was very touching. He hugged me, my sister-- loved us from the first moment. And we reciprocated. We fell in love with him. He took my hand-- we had no luggage-- [SOBBING] and we walked. It was a small town, a very tiny town. We walked to his house. And I remember the stench of farm animals. And I think my first question to my grandfather was if there are cows around. [LAUGHS] I can smell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shmuel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, yes. And it was so quiet. And we got to the house, and I met my grandmother. I met my father's sister. My grandmother's name was Sara, and my aunt's name was Tzivye. And my cousin-- they had one child, Sheindl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a year younger than I, so she was eight. I was nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
